pairs.a_topic: Geographic Expansion Sequence: Dominate Local, Then Expand
pairs.b_topic: Geographic Monopoly: Fix State Before Expanding
pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi argues that a local service or coaching business must fully dominate its immediate geographic market before attempting national or online expansion. He uses the analogy that armies that over-expand before fortifying their base collapse, and specifically told a home-flipping coach generating roughly $4M/year in Fayetteville and Raleigh that the two-city market alone could support $100M
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi recommends that a business at ~50% market share in a single dominant geography should prioritize increasing share within that geography to 70-80% before expanding to other states, because the share-capture path has no equivalent elsewhere and directly starves competitors.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi argues that serially jumping from opportunity to opportunity at the first sign of difficulty is the primary reason entrepreneurs fail to achieve large outcomes: each jump restarts the cycle at uninformed optimism, meaning the entrepreneur never passes through informed pessimism and the valley of despair to reach the upside on any single path.

pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi claims that the specificity of instruction required is inversely correlated with the skill level of the recipient: a low-skill person needs every sub-step explained (e.g., what a browser is, how to type), while a high-skill person can execute from a single high-level directive. Entrepreneurs misidentify this as a motivation problem when it is actually a skill-gap problem.
